text: Hi guys, today I'll be presenting on laparoscopic intracorporeal colon colonic anastomosis. Patient underwent left colon cancer surgery by the surgical oncologist and I'm doing an intracorporeal
The surgeon performs the final stages of the resection, with the transverse colon being maneuvered down towards the descending colon-sigmoid junction, aligning the two segments for anastomosis.
If you have to do a mini laparotomy and outside anastomosis, we'll have to make quite a bit of an incision and also that mis-entry would be under tension.

The surgeon places a stay stitch to assess the alignment of the anastomosis, then positions a stapler to evaluate its fit. Entratomies are made on the transverse colon and descending colon to facilitate the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses an L hook to create precise enterotomies in the transverse and descending colon, preparing for the intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a mirror line to open the enterotomy along the tinea coli for the anastomosis, preparing the bowel for connection.
The surgeon adjusts the tissue to ensure the opening is just large enough for the stapler to be inserted, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon selects a blue load stapler for use on the colon, noting the soft texture of the colon tissue, which makes the blue stapler an adequate choice for this part of the procedure.
The surgeon uses the entire length of a 60 cm stapler to perform the anastomosis, with the stapled tissue visibly aligning and coming together smoothly.
The surgeon closes the defect using a stapler, following a decision to opt for staple closure over suture closure.
Here, unfortunately, the video was disconnected and we had to recharge, so part of the video will be missing here.
The surgeon places three sutures, followed by an anastomosis using a stapler, completing the connection between the bowel segments.
The video shows the completed stapling of the colon, with two staple lines positioned close to each other. The surgeon reinforces these staple lines with limbered sutures for added security.
This doesn't usually take much time, which adds extra ten minutes to the operative procedure, but gives me the peace that the stapling is reinforced. Theoretically, this is not required.
If the stapling all went well, you really don't need to, but given the advantage of laparoscopy and suturing, it just gives me a feel that the entire stapling is nicely reinforced.
Taking the idea that we have in the open surgery where we reinforce the staple line adequately.

Here there are two staple lines next to each other. That is a concern for some surgeons but we have seen in multiple gastrointestinal procedures that the two staple lines close to each other does not cause any ischemia to the bowel itself.
But definitely reinforcing the staple line with a suture gives you the peace of mind at night as a surgeon so I ended up choosing to close this suture line.

The patient did very well after surgery and was discharged home three days after surgery itself with a return of bowel function quickly. The specimen was removed through a fan still incision that also helped with the pain. There were more than twelve lymph nodes retrieved in the specimen.
